description: The provided image pixel displays a publisher status badge with no extractable scientific chart or micrograph. Relying on the accompanying paper context regarding Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-(0, 3, 6, 9)Cr austenitic low-density steel, the investigated figure likely presents data from near-rapid solidified strips in as-cast and annealed (550°C, 5 h) states. The alloy system consists of an austenite matrix with δ-ferrite increasing from 10.2 vol% to 19.6 vol% as Cr rises, accompanied by suppressed nano-scale κ-carbides, DO3 ordering in ferrite, and Cr23C6 boundary precipitation in 9Cr. Mechanical trends show as-cast yield strengths of 724, 617, 652, and 740 MPa for 0Cr through 9Cr, with annealed 6Cr achieving the highest elongation of 44.3 ± 3%. Strain hardening rates progressively increase with Cr content, while annealing reduces dislocation density and refines slip band spacing, underscoring the trade-off between carbide suppression and ferrite stabilization.

This figure presents stacked X-ray diffraction patterns of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9) low-density steel strips, labeled 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr, recorded over a 2θ range of 30° to 100°. Each curve identifies the coexisting austenite (γ) and ferrite (δ) phases, with indexed reflections including γ(111), δ(110), γ(200), δ(200), γ(220), δ(211), γ(311), and γ(222). Visual comparison reveals that the δ(110) peak intensity at approximately 44.1° progressively increases with Cr content, while austenite remains the major matrix phase across all compositions. The caption and related text report that increasing Cr addition promotes δ-ferrite formation, raising ferrite volume fraction from 10.2% in 0Cr to 19.6% in 9Cr, consistent with a FA (ferrite-to-austenite) solidification mode. Additionally, austenite lattice constants determined by JADE are cited as 3.70 Å, 3.56 Å, 3.56 Å, and 3.55 Å for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr, respectively. This XRD evidence establishes that Cr significantly stabilizes δ-ferrite in the near-rapidly solidified microstructure.

This figure presents four optical micrographs (OM) of as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C–xCr low-density steel strips, with subfigures (a)–(d) corresponding to 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr, respectively; all images share a 20 μm scale bar. The dark-etched δ-ferrite phase is uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. With increasing Cr content, the ferrite morphology visibly transitions from rod-like to block-like, and the quantitative data reported in the caption indicates that the ferrite fraction rises from 10.2 vol% for 0Cr to 11.4 vol%, 14.8 vol%, and 19.6 vol% for 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr, respectively. In Fig. 2(a), ferrite particles reside at the centers of austenite grains, suggesting that δ-ferrite nucleated first from the melt during near-rapid solidification. These metallographic observations support the reported FA (ferrite-to-austenite) solidification mode (L → L+δ → L+δ+γ → δ+γ) and demonstrate that Cr addition promotes ferrite formation and progressively changes its morphology, providing the microstructural basis for interpreting the alloy's mechanical behavior.

Figure 3 presents tensile mechanical data for as-cast Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%, labeled 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr) austenitic low-density steel strips produced by near-rapid solidification. Subfigure (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves: 0Cr exhibits the lowest yield strength (~724 MPa) but the highest total elongation (~40.8%); as Cr increases, yield and ultimate tensile strengths rise progressively, reaching ~740 MPa and ~979 MPa for 9Cr, while ductility drops sharply to ~20.3%. Subfigure (b) overlays true stress–strain curves (left axis) with corresponding strain-hardening-rate curves (right axis). The strain hardening rate increases with Cr content (0Cr ≈1626 MPa, 9Cr ≈2385 MPa), and the Considère intersections shift to lower true strains for higher-Cr alloys, reflecting reduced uniform elongation. Collectively, the data demonstrate that Cr addition strengthens the steel via enhanced strain hardening and solid-solution effects but trades off ductility, with 9Cr showing the highest strength yet earliest fracture.

This figure presents TEM bright-field (BF) and dark-field (DF) images together with selected-area diffraction patterns (SADPs) revealing the austenite microstructure in near-rapid solidified Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) low-density steel strips. Subfigures (a)–(d) show BF images of 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr, and 9Cr strips respectively, with corresponding SADPs inset. As Cr content increases, the dislocation density in austenite progressively rises from sparse dislocations in 0Cr to dense networks in 6Cr, while the 9Cr strip shows slightly lower density than 6Cr as caption reports. Concurrently, the κ-carbide superlattice visible in the 0Cr SADP weakens in 3Cr and 6Cr and disappears entirely in 9Cr, indicating Cr suppresses κ-carbide precipitation. Subfigure (e) is a DF image of the 0Cr strip using the (010)κ reflection, resolving κ-carbides localized at the austenite/ferrite phase boundary. Subfigure (f) displays BF image and SADP of the 9Cr strip, revealing Cr23C6 carbides along austenite grain boundaries; caption reports these have a cubic structure with a lattice constant of 1.054 nm and widths of about 100 nm. These observations demonstrate that Cr addition elevates dislocation density and inhibits κ-carbide formation, yet promotes detrimental Cr23C6 grain-boundary precipitation at higher contents.

Figure 5 presents TEM dark-field (DF) images and corresponding selected-area electron diffraction patterns (SADPs) of ferrite in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C–xCr austenitic low-density steel strips subjected to near-rapid solidification and annealing at 550 °C for 5 h. Subfigures (a) and (b) show DF images of the 0Cr and 3Cr strips, respectively, formed using δ-ferrite reflections ((110)δ and (1̄10)δ) with [111]δ zone-axis SADPs, exhibiting clean ferrite morphology without superlattice reflections. In contrast, subfigures (c) and (d) display DF images of the 6Cr and 9Cr strips, respectively, acquired using the (200)DO3 reflection, with composite SADPs along [001]δ+[001]DO3 and [011]δ+[011]DO3 zone axes. The images explicitly labeled 'δ+DO3' and the indexed superlattice spots confirm the presence of nano-scale DO3 precipitates within the δ-ferrite of the 6Cr and 9Cr alloys. The figure demonstrates that increasing Cr content promotes the formation of ordered DO3 phase in ferrite, whereas the ferrite in 0Cr and 3Cr strips remains essentially free of such secondary phases.

Figure 6 presents X-ray diffraction patterns of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) austenitic low-density steel strips after annealing at 550°C for 5 h, comparing phase constitutions across Cr contents. Four stacked curves are shown for 0Cr (black), 3Cr (blue), 6Cr (red), and 9Cr (green). All samples exhibit prominent austenite (γ) peaks indexed as (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), together with ferrite (δ) peaks including (110), (200), and (211), confirming the dual-phase γ + δ structure. Notably, the annealed 0Cr strip shows a distinct κ-carbide (111) reflection near 41.4°, indicating pronounced κ-carbide precipitation during annealing. In contrast, this κ-carbide peak is absent in the Cr-containing strips (3Cr, 6Cr, 9Cr), demonstrating that Cr addition effectively suppresses κ-carbide precipitation even after annealing. The figure supports the conclusion that Cr stabilizes the austenite-ferrite matrix while inhibiting undesirable κ-carbide formation.

Figure 7 comprises four SEM micrographs (a–d) of Fe–28Mn–9Al-1.2C-xCr low-density steel strips after annealing at 550°C for 5 h, with Cr contents of (a) 0Cr, (b) 3Cr, (c) 6Cr, and (d) 9Cr; each image includes a 20 μm scale bar. In (a), grain boundaries and a dendritic solidification structure are prominently visible across the microstructure. In (b), (c), and (d), the matrix contains dispersed second-phase particles exhibiting short rod-like, triangular, and irregular morphologies, while grain boundaries are no longer clearly observed as Cr content increases. The caption and text state that the constituent phases remain austenite and ferrite, and that Cr addition improves corrosion resistance, which explains the absence of visible grain boundaries in the Cr-containing samples. The progression from (a) to (d) illustrates that Cr suppresses grain-boundary etching contrast and modifies the size, shape, and area fraction of the second phase, supporting the core conclusion that Cr strongly influences microstructural stability and corrosion resistance while inhibiting κ-carbide precipitation.

Figure 8 presents the tensile properties of Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9) austenitic low-density steel strips after annealing at 550°C for 5 h, comprising two panels. Panel (a) displays engineering stress–strain curves, where the 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr and 9Cr samples are represented by solid black, dashed red, dotted blue and dash-dotted cyan lines, respectively. Panel (b) shows the corresponding true stress–strain curves (left y-axis, MPa) together with strain hardening rate curves (right y-axis, MPa). Visually, increasing Cr content raises the strain hardening rate, with 9Cr exhibiting the highest rate and 6Cr displaying superior uniform elongation among the annealed alloys. The caption and citation text report that, compared with the as-cast condition, the strain hardening rate of all annealed strips except 9Cr clearly decreases, with the 3Cr strip showing the largest reduction of about 500 MPa. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that moderate Cr addition coupled with annealing enhances the strength–ductility balance, whereas excessive Cr improves strength and work-hardening capacity at the expense of ductility.

This figure presents TEM characterization of austenite in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) low-density steel strips annealed at 550°C for 5 h. Subfigures (a)–(d) are bright-field (BF) images with corresponding selected-area diffraction patterns (SADPs) for 0Cr, 3Cr, 6Cr and 9Cr strips, respectively, showing dislocation networks in the austenite matrix (yellow arrows). The SADPs reveal clear κ-carbide superlattice reflections in 0Cr, weak superlattices in 3Cr and 6Cr, and no superlattice in 9Cr. Subfigure (e) is a dark-field (DF) image of the 0Cr strip along (010)κ, highlighting nano-scale κ-carbide precipitates. Subfigure (f) is a DF image of the 9Cr strip along (1̄44) of Cr23C6 with its SADP, identifying coarse Cr23C6 carbides at grain boundaries; citation reports these carbides are about 200 nm wide. The data demonstrate that increasing Cr suppresses κ-carbide precipitation while promoting boundary Cr23C6 formation, and that annealing reduces dislocation density in Cr-containing strips, providing direct microstructural evidence for the mechanical property trends.

This figure presents four dark-field (DF) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) micrographs showing ferrite morphology, with each panel including an inset selected-area electron diffraction pattern (SADP). The caption identifies the material as Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-xCr (x = 0, 3, 6, 9 wt%) austenitic low-density steel strips annealed at 550 °C for 5 h, where (a) and (b) are 0Cr and 3Cr strips imaged with a δ-ferrite reflection and (c) and (d) are 6Cr and 9Cr strips imaged with a DO3 reflection. The SADPs are indexed as superimposed [001] or [011] zone axes of δ-ferrite and the ordered DO3 phase, confirming their coexistence. The 500 nm scale-bar images reveal nanoscale granular contrast within the ferrite regions, attributed to DO3 ordering. These results demonstrate that the DO3 superlattice forms in the δ-ferrite of all annealed strips independent of Cr content, supporting the conclusion that this annealing treatment universally induces a δ + DO3 phase assembly in the ferrite.

Figure 11 presents engineering stress–strain curves and bright-field (BF) TEM images of austenite in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C–3Cr austenitic low-density steel after annealing at 550 °C for 1 h, 5 h, and 9 h, alongside an as-cast benchmark. Panel (a) plots engineering stress (MPa) versus engineering strain (%), revealing that yield strength increases substantially after 1 h annealing but shows no significant change at 5 h and 9 h, whereas plasticity rises progressively with prolonged annealing time. Panels (b), (c), and (d) provide BF TEM images of the 1 h, 5 h, and 9 h specimens, respectively, with scale bars of 500 nm, 500 nm, and 100 nm; these micrographs show a clear decline in dislocation density as annealing time increases, and panel (d) additionally captures nano-scale precipitates reported as κ-carbides embedded in the austenite matrix. Collectively, the figure supports the conclusion that κ-carbide precipitation is largely completed within the first hour and contributes to the elevated yield strength, while extended annealing drives recovery and reduces dislocation density to enhance ductility; the caption notes that overly prolonged treatment may coarsen κ-carbides, so the annealing time should be optimized to balance strength and plasticity.

Figure 12 presents four TEM micrographs showing slip bands in Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C-(0,6)Cr austenitic low-density steel strips after tensile fracture. Subfigure (a) displays the as-cast 0Cr strip with an average slip-band spacing of ~90 nm, whereas (b) shows the annealed 0Cr strip with a widened spacing of ~190 nm. Subfigure (c) depicts the as-cast 6Cr strip with a spacing of ~80 nm, and (d) shows the annealed 6Cr strip with a refined spacing of ~60 nm. The divergent evolution upon annealing—spacing increases in 0Cr but decreases in 6Cr—correlates with opposite changes in elongation. The accompanying text reports that average slip-band widths are 13.4 nm and 12.5 nm for as-cast 0Cr and 6Cr, and 20.5 nm and 7.9 nm after annealing, respectively. The authors attribute the coarser spacing in annealed 0Cr to precipitation of ~14 nm κ-carbides that obstruct dislocations, while finer, less abundant κ-carbides in 6Cr facilitate multi-directional shearing and denser slip bands. These micrographs support that slip-band spacing is governed by κ-carbide precipitation state and initial dislocation density, controlling strain hardening and ductility.

Figure 13 is a multi-axis line chart showing the slip band spacing, slip band width, and total elongation after tensile fracture for Fe–28Mn–9Al–1.2C austenitic low-density steel strips in three conditions: as-cast 0Cr, as-cast 6Cr, and annealed 6Cr (550°C for 5 h). The black-square curve (left y-axis) represents slip band spacing, decreasing from approximately 90 nm for as-cast 0Cr to about 80 nm for as-cast 6Cr and further to roughly 60 nm for annealed 6Cr. The red-triangle curve (magenta right y-axis) denotes slip band width, following a similar downward trend from about 13 nm to 12 nm and then to approximately 8 nm. The blue-circle curve (blue right y-axis) indicates total elongation, displaying a V-shaped variation: it falls from approximately 42% for as-cast 0Cr to a minimum of about 32% for as-cast 6Cr before rising to the highest value of roughly 44% after annealing. These trends demonstrate that although finer slip bands and smaller spacing generally correspond to higher ductility via dynamic slip band refinement, the as-cast 6Cr strip breaks this correlation because of its high pre-existing dislocation density; annealing reduces the defect density and re-establishes the expected relationship, producing the finest slip bands and greatest total elongation.
